title: Ensure public network access is disabled

input_row.title: Ensure public network access is disabled
input_row.description: Disable public network access to prevent exposure to the internet and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. Use private endpoints to securely manage access within trusted networks.
input_row.rationale: Disabling public network access improves security by ensuring that the service is not directly exposed to the public Internet. This has the added benefit of providing more granular control over security settings and configurations for those additional layers of separation.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Networking. 6. Under Inbound traffic configuration, ensure that Public network access is set to Disabled. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list apps: az webapp list For each app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az webapp deployment slot list --resource-group --name For each deployment slot, ensure that publicNetworkAccess is set to Disabled. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 701a595d-38fb-4a66-ae6d-fb3735217622 - Name: 'App Service app slots should disable public network access'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Networking. 6. Under Inbound traffic configuration, click the text next to Public network access. 7. Select the radio button next to Disabled. 8. Click Save. 9. Check the box to confirm the change. 10. Click Continue. 11. Repeat steps 1-10 for each app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command to disable public network access: az resource update --resource-group --name /slots/ --resource-type "Microsoft.Web/sites" -- set properties.publicNetworkAccess=Disabled
input_row.impact: NOTE: Prior to disabling public network access, it is strongly recommended that, for each App Service deployment slot, either: • complete virtual network integration as described in "Ensure app is integrated with a virtual network" OR • set up private endpoints/links as described in "Ensure private endpoints are used to access App Service apps." Disabling public network access restricts direct access to the service. This enhances security but will require the configuration of a virtual network and/or private endpoints for any services or users needing access within trusted networks.
input_row.default_value: By default, public network access is enabled.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The description clearly discusses disabling public network access, which is a standard security control that can be queried via AWS APIs.
- The rationale and references justify the need for such a control and indicate that AWS has specific configurations related to network access that can be audited.
- The public network access checks fall under network configurations that can be queried automatically.
- There are AWS services and configurations similar to Azure's public network access that can be reflected and audited using the AWS APIs. For example, the AWS API for Amazon EC2 and AWS Lambda has various checks that can be automated to see if instances or functions have public accessibility disabled.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_ec2_instance_public_access_disabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use the `boto3` library to query the EC2 instances.
   - Call `ec2.describe_instances()` to retrieve a list of instances and their network configurations.
   - Check the `PublicIpAddress` field for each instance. If this field is not `None`, the instance has public access.
  
2. If applicable, also check the security group configurations associated with the instances using `ec2.describe_security_groups()`.
   - Look for inbound rules that allow access from `0.0.0.0/0` which indicates public access is allowed.

3. Use the AWS CLI to further validate these security settings:
   ```bash
   # List all EC2 instances and check for public IP addresses
   aws ec2 describe-instances --query "Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId, PublicIpAddress]"
   
   # Check security group rules
   aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[*].[GroupId, IpPermissions[*]]"
   ```

4. **Pass/Fail criteria**:
   - Instances with `PublicIpAddress` set to `None` and security groups with no inbound rules permitting `0.0.0.0/0` are compliant (Pass).
   - Instances with any `PublicIpAddress` present or security groups allowing `0.0.0.0/0` are non-compliant (Fail).

By automating the checks for public access configurations in EC2 instances, organizations can ensure adherence to security best practices as stipulated in frameworks like CIS and maintain a robust compliance posture in their AWS cloud environments.
